navigator.name: Jean-François de Galaup, comte de La Pérouse
navigator.bio: French naval officer chosen by Louis XVI to lead France's answer to Cook: a scientific circumnavigation aboard La Boussole and L'Astrolabe (1785–1788). The expedition crossed the Pacific from Chile to Alaska, China, Kamchatka and Samoa, reached Botany Bay days after the First Fleet — and vanished. Decades later, islander accounts collected by Peter Dillon placed both wrecks at Vanikoro.

voyage.title: The Voyage of La Pérouse (1785–1788)
voyage.purpose: A scientific circumnavigation to complete Cook's work: chart the Pacific from the Americas to Asia, open trade reconnaissance, and carry savants, artists and instruments to the ends of the earth.
voyage.summary: Departing Brest in August 1785, La Pérouse rounded Cape Horn, refitted in Chile, called at Easter Island and Hawaii, lost twenty-one men in Alaska, crossed to Macao and Kamchatka — whence his journals travelled overland to Paris — lost twelve more at Samoa, and reached Botany Bay in January 1788 as the First Fleet anchored there. Sailing north in March 1788, the expedition was never seen again by Europeans: the end is reconstructed from islander accounts placing both wrecks at Vanikoro.
voyage.approved_via: Magna Carta submission #3 — proposed, gate-checked, curator-verified and approved by the editor-in-chief on 2026-07-21

waypoints.date_note: The expedition sailed from Brest on 1 August 1785.
waypoints.event: La Boussole and L'Astrolabe sail from Brest with over 200 men, including savants, artists and gardeners — France's answer to Cook.
waypoints.diary_excerpt: The expedition sailed from Brest rather sooner than had at first been contemplated, on August 1, 1785, and doubled Cape Horn in January of the following year.

waypoints.event: After doubling Cape Horn the ships refitted on the coast of Chile, where La Pérouse befriended Ambrosio O'Higgins — whose son Bernardo would one day liberate the country.
waypoints.diary_excerpt: Laperouse at once conceived an attachment for O'Higgins, "a man of extraordinary activity," and one "adored in the country."

waypoints.event: A brief call at Easter Island; the French left gifts and seeds, but relations were strained by persistent theft.
waypoints.diary_excerpt: In April, 1786, the expedition was at Easter Island, where the inhabitants appeared to be a set of cunning and hypocritical thieves

waypoints.date_note: Scott dates the landfall broadly to May 1786; the landing on Maui is usually placed at 29–30 May — La Pérouse was the first European to set foot on the island.
waypoints.event: First European landing on Maui. La Pérouse refused to claim the island for France, writing sharply against the European habit of annexing inhabited lands.
waypoints.diary_excerpt: Steering north, the Sandwich Islands were reached early in May. Here Laperouse liked the people

waypoints.date_note: The disaster at the bay's entrance struck on 13 July 1786.
waypoints.event: Charting the Alaskan coast, the expedition anchored in a bay La Pérouse named Port des Français. On 13 July two boats were swallowed by the tidal race at its entrance: twenty-one men drowned, and a cenotaph was raised on what is still called Cenotaph Island.
Ten days at the Spanish presidio and missions of Monterey — the first non-Spanish visitors to Alta California, observing the missions with a sharp, sceptical eye.
Across the Pacific to the Portuguese enclave of Macao, where the sea-otter furs collected in Alaska were sold for the benefit of the crews.

After charting the fog-bound coasts of Tartary, Sakhalin and the Kurils — where a strait still bears his ship's name — the expedition rested at Petropavlovsk, and the voyage's records went home overland with young Lesseps, the only member ever to return to Europe.
waypoints.diary_excerpt: At Petropavlovsk in Kamchatka the drawings of the artists and the journals of the commander up to date were packed up, and sent to France overland across Asiatic Russia, in charge of a young member of the staff, J. B. B. de Lesseps.

waypoints.date_note: The massacre at the watering place struck on 11 December 1787.
waypoints.event: At Tutuila a watering party was attacked: Captain de Langle — commander of the Astrolabe — and eleven others were killed; the boats that stayed afloat saved forty-nine of the sixty-one ashore.
waypoints.diary_excerpt: happily remaining afloat, were enabled to save forty-nine men out of the sixty-one.

waypoints.date_note: The French stood off the heads on 24–25 January and anchored on the 26th — the very day Phillip shifted the First Fleet to Port Jackson.
waypoints.event: Arriving off Botany Bay, the French sighted the British First Fleet at anchor within. For six weeks the two expeditions shared the bay in courtesy; Père Receveur, wounded at Samoa, died here, and his tomb still stands.
waypoints.diary_excerpt: This was a British squadron, at anchor in Botany Bay, the pennants and ensigns of which we could plainly distinguish.
waypoints.diary_source_citation: Ernest Scott, Laperouse (1912) — quoting La Pérouse's journal

waypoints.date_note: The ships left Botany Bay on 10 March 1788 and were never seen again by Europeans. The end of the voyage is reconstructed from islander accounts collected by Peter Dillon in 1826–1827, which place both wrecks at Vanikoro.
waypoints.event: The secret of the sea: both ships were lost on the reefs of Vanikoro. Survivors built a small craft from the wreck and sailed away — they were never heard of again.
waypoints.diary_excerpt: They told me that the natives of Mannicolo stated that many years ago two large ships arrived at their islands; one anchored at the island of Whanoo, and the other at the island of Paiou, a little distance from each other.
waypoints.diary_source_citation: Ernest Scott, Laperouse (1912) — quoting Peter Dillon
